Forecasting UK Inflation with Prophet

MTH6139 Time Series Coursework 1

Kardelen Karaoglan

March 2026

1. Introduction

This project looks at UK inflation by using Meta’s Prophet forecasting tool in R. The analysis uses the Consumer Prices Index (CPI) yearly rate for all items, taken from the Office for National Statistics (ONS).

Inflation is an interesting time series to study because it reflects what is happening in the wider economy. It can be stable for long stretches, then shift quickly when something major happens. The COVID-19 pandemic is a good example - it caused sharp disruptions to prices and supply chains, followed by a period of unusually high inflation. This makes inflation a good test case for Prophet, since the data is not always smooth or predictable.

The report starts by introducing the data and looking at its main features through graphs. It then covers how the data is prepared for Prophet, fits a basic model, and compares it with a few alternative versions. The final sections discuss the results, the model’s limitations, and the main takeaways.

2. The dataset

The dataset is monthly UK inflation from the ONS specifically, the CPI annual rate for all items. It runs from January 1989 to January 2026, which means it covers some genuinely dramatic periods in economic history.

This dataset works well for the project because it is recorded consistently over time and matters in the real world. Inflation is shaped by economic shifts, government decisions, and unexpected events, so it does not follow a neat, predictable path. That makes it a realistic and slightly tricky test for Prophet.

The data needs a bit of cleaning before it can be used. The original file has extra information and a mix of annual and monthly figures, so the monthly values need to be pulled out and put into the right format for Prophet.

3. Exploratory analysis

3.1 Time series plot

The first thing to do is just look at the data. Plotting the full series straight away gives a good sense of how inflation has behaved over the past 35 or so years.

Figure 1. UK CPI annual inflation rate, 1989 to 2026

The plot immediately tells a story. Inflation starts high in the early 1990s (around 7–8%), then gradually settles into a calmer period through the late 1990s and early 2000s (between 1–3%). Then come the disruptions: a rise around 2008, another around 2011, each one a fingerprint of the economic turbulence happening at the time.

Then comes the spike that is impossible to ignore, inflation surging past 10% in the early 2020s, a level not seen in decades, before falling back sharply. It is a stark reminder that inflation does not move in neat, predictable ways. For a model like Prophet, which prefers smooth, gradual patterns, this kind of volatility is a real challenge.

3.2 Moving average

To get a clearer view of the overall trend, a 12-month moving average is applied. This smooths out the month-to-month noise and makes the bigger picture easier to see.

Figure 2. UK CPI annual inflation rate with 12-month moving average

With the short-term noise smoothed out, the trend becomes much clearer. Inflation starts high, gradually settles into a long stable period, then rises sharply in the early 2020s before falling back again.

It is clear that inflation does not behave in one consistent way, it really depends on what is going on in the economy at the time.

3.3 Distribution of inflation

A histogram gives a different view of the data by showing how the inflation values are spread out overall, rather than over time. It helps to see where most values sit and whether there are any extreme ones.

Figure 3. Distribution of UK CPI annual inflation

The histogram shows that inflation is not concentrated around a single narrow level. Most values fall within a low-to-moderate range, but there are also some much higher observations. This suggests that inflation occasionally experiences more unusual periods, which matches the sharp movements seen in the time series plot.

3.4 Inflation by calendar month

A boxplot split by calendar month helps to check whether inflation tends to be higher or lower at certain times of year. If there is a seasonal pattern, it should show up as clear differences between the months.

Figure 4. UK CPI annual inflation by calendar month

The boxplots do not really show a clear seasonal pattern. The differences between months are pretty small compared to the bigger swings we saw over time. This suggests seasonality is not a major driver here, which is worth keeping in mind when we look at Prophet’s seasonal component later on.

3.5 Summary statistics

To round off the exploratory analysis, some summary statistics help to put numbers to what the plots have already shown.

Table 1. Summary statistics for UK CPI annual inflation
Statistic Value
Minimum -0.10000
1st quartile 1.50000
Median 2.30000
Mean 2.83618
3rd quartile 3.20000
Maximum 11.10000

The statistics confirm that inflation varies quite a lot, ranging from just below 0% all the way up to 11.1%. Most values sit between around 1.5% and 3%, but that maximum is a reminder of how extreme things can get. Any forecasts based on this data should be taken with a degree of caution.

4. Preparing the data for Prophet

Prophet is fairly easy to use, but it does need the data in a specific format - a data frame with just two columns: ds for the dates and y for the values.

The raw ONS file is not in this format, so a bit of wrangling is needed. The extra rows are removed, the annual figures are filtered out, and the monthly values are put into a clean data frame ready to use.

Table 2. First and last monthly observations in the Prophet-ready dataset
ds y
1989-01-01 4.9
1989-02-01 5
1989-03-01 5
… …
2025-11-01 3.2
2025-12-01 3.4
2026-01-01 3

After cleaning, the final dataset runs from January 1989 to January 2026 and is ready to be used with Prophet.

5. Baseline Prophet model

The first model is a straightforward baseline using the full dataset. The idea is to see what Prophet does with all the available data before trying anything more involved.

The model is set to forecast 24 months ahead, so two years beyond the last data point.

Figure 5. Baseline Prophet forecast for UK CPI annual inflation

The forecast plot shows that Prophet does a decent job of tracking the general direction of inflation, but it smooths over some of the sharper changes. The spike in the early 2020s is the clearest example - the actual data rises far more steeply than the model’s fitted trend. This highlights a key limitation: Prophet works well for gradual changes but struggles with sudden, large movements.

Figure 6. Prophet trend and seasonal components for UK CPI annual inflation

The components plot breaks the forecast down into trend and seasonality. The trend component does most of the work here — the seasonal component is quite small, which is consistent with the earlier finding that inflation does not have a strong seasonal pattern.

Table 3. Final 12 months of the baseline Prophet forecast
ds yhat yhat_lower yhat_upper
2027-02-01 4.353949 1.998323 6.646885
2027-03-01 4.409559 2.039974 6.624662
2027-04-01 4.505957 2.129716 6.920386
2027-05-01 4.467341 2.321215 6.656657
2027-06-01 4.425069 2.286156 6.787581
2027-07-01 4.405053 2.103629 6.773595
2027-08-01 4.482818 2.185506 6.473581
2027-09-01 4.522797 2.188593 6.825175
2027-10-01 4.511232 2.242510 6.871304
2027-11-01 4.537777 2.338509 6.671425
2027-12-01 4.582946 2.249191 6.878573
2028-01-01 4.504422 2.394060 6.736027

The table shows the last part of the forecast with the upper and lower uncertainty bounds included. The fairly wide range of these bounds is a reminder that even a well-fitted model cannot tell us exactly where inflation is going.

6. Alternative Prophet models

To check how much the results depend on the choices made, the baseline model is compared against two alternatives - one using a shorter time period and one with seasonality removed.

6.1 Prophet on post-2000 data

One concern with the baseline model is that data from the late 1980s and early 1990s might be pulling the trend in a direction that is no longer relevant. To test this, a second model is fitted using only data from January 2000 onwards, so it focuses on more recent inflation behaviour.

Figure 7. Prophet forecast for UK CPI annual inflation using post-2000 data

By starting from 2000, the model puts more weight on how inflation has behaved in recent decades. This seems reasonable, since the economic environment has changed quite a bit since the early 1990s and older data might not be as useful for forecasting today.

6.2 Prophet without yearly seasonality

Since the earlier analysis suggested that inflation does not have a strong seasonal pattern, a third model is fitted with yearly seasonality switched off, to see whether it makes much of a difference.

Figure 8. Prophet forecast for UK CPI annual inflation without yearly seasonality

If the results are similar to the baseline, it would suggest that the seasonal component is not adding much and that the trend is doing most of the work.

7. Forecast comparison

With all three models fitted, they can now be compared to see how much the forecasts actually differ depending on the data used and how seasonality is handled.

Figure 9. Comparison of Prophet forecasts for UK CPI annual inflation

All three forecasts follow a broadly similar shape, but there are some differences. The post-2000 model is noticeably influenced by more recent inflation behaviour, while removing seasonality makes very little difference to the forecast.

This suggests that the time period used to train the model has a bigger effect on the results than how seasonality is handled.

8. Discussion

Overall, Prophet handles the broad movement of inflation reasonably well, especially the gradual shifts in level over time. The fact that all three models produce a similar general pattern is encouraging — it suggests the main trend is a real feature of the data rather than just an artefact of one particular set of choices.

That said, Prophet clearly struggles with sudden changes. The spike in the early 2020s is the most obvious example — none of the models capture it well. This is a genuine limitation of Prophet when applied to economic data, where unexpected events can cause rapid shifts that no model trained on past patterns could really anticipate.

The alternative models also show that the time period used has a bigger effect on the results than the seasonal settings. Using post-2000 data leads to noticeably different forecasts, while removing seasonality makes almost no difference. This backs up the earlier finding that seasonality is not a major factor in this inflation series.

Overall, Prophet is a useful tool for understanding the general direction of inflation, but its forecasts should be treated with caution — particularly during periods when inflation is changing quickly.

9. Conclusion

This project used Prophet to forecast UK inflation and looked at how different modelling choices affect the results. The baseline model was a good starting point — it captured the overall trend well but smoothed over some of the more extreme movements in the data.

The alternative models showed that the time period used matters more than how seasonality is set up. Focusing on post-2000 data gave forecasts that better reflect how inflation has behaved in recent years.

Prophet is a solid and fairly accessible tool, especially for picking up broad trends over time. It is not perfect — it struggles when inflation moves suddenly — but used alongside some economic understanding, it gives a useful picture of where things might be heading. The main takeaway from this project is probably that no model can predict everything, and being honest about that is just as important as the forecasts themselves.

10 References

The inflation series used in this project was obtained from the Office for National Statistics (ONS) time series database.

Forecasts were produced in R using Meta’s Prophet.
